items.headline: Wall Street Ends Mixed Amid Fed Anticipation and Oil Price Swings
items.summary:
U.S. equities closed mixed on Tuesday, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average hitting a new record while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite pulled back as investors awaited the Federal Reserve's policy decision. Falling oil prices weighed on energy stocks, and AI-related shares experienced notable volatility.

Pre-market trading on Wednesday saw stock futures inching higher, with S&P 500 contracts slightly in the red and Nasdaq futures showing modest gains, as Wall Street prepared for the Fed's announcement.

items.statContext: ⬆️ +0.6% (+328.64 points), marking a new record high.
⬇️ -1.2% (-307.60 points). Early Wednesday, it was 0.3% higher.

items.headline: La-Z-Boy Rises on Strong Earnings
items.summary: Shares of La-Z-Boy ($LZB) surged +20% after the furniture maker reported stronger-than-expected FQ4 results, with adjusted EPS rising 37% year-over-year and revenue slightly ahead of estimates. The company also announced a new $300M share repurchase program.

items.headline: SpaceX Continues Rally, Surpasses Amazon Valuation
items.summary: SpaceX ($SPCX) climbed +4.8% on Wednesday, extending its rally after announcing a $60 billion deal to acquire AI coding startup Cursor. This move helped SpaceX briefly surpass Microsoft and end the session above Amazon, becoming the fifth-most valuable U.S. company.

items.headline: Jabil Jumps on Strong Q3 Results and AI Demand
items.summary: Jabil ($JBL) jumped +12.4% after reporting stronger results for the latest quarter than analysts expected, with CEO Mike Dastoor highlighting 'extremely strong' AI infrastructure demand.

items.headline: Marvell Technology Leads Chipmaker Pullback
items.summary: Marvell Technology ($MRVL) dropped -9.78% as chipmakers pulled back after recent gains, contributing to a broader tech sector decline.

items.headline: Micron Technology Falls Amid Tech Sector Weakness
items.summary: Micron Technology ($MU) declined -6.2%, participating in the broader tech and AI stock sell-off that weighed on the market.

items.headline: Energy Sector ETF Declines with Oil Prices
items.summary: The Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF ($XLE) fell -0.3% as crude oil prices plummeted following reports of a U.S.-Iran agreement.

items.headline: Federal Reserve Concludes June Meeting, Rate Decision Expected
items.summary: The Federal Reserve concludes its two-day June meeting today, with a rate decision at 2:00 p.m. ET and Chair Kevin Warsh's first press conference at 2:30 p.m. ET. Markets widely anticipate no change to the federal funds rate, currently at 3.50%-3.75%, with focus on Warsh's commentary regarding inflation and the economic outlook.

items.headline: Oil Prices Plummet on Iran Deal Optimism
items.summary: Crude oil prices saw a significant drop on Tuesday, with Brent crude futures falling below $80 per barrel for the first time since March. This decline followed reports of a tentative agreement between Iran and the United States, which could allow Iran to resume oil exports and ease global supply concerns.

items.headline: Sector Rotation Sees Shift from Tech to Cyclicals
items.summary: Investors exhibited a rotation out of high-valuation technology shares and into economically sensitive sectors. Financial stocks gained 1.5% and industrials advanced 0.7%, while the Information Technology sector was the weakest performer, dropping 2.3%. Healthcare and smaller-cap stocks are also showing signs of improving relative strength.

items.headline: Jabil Reports Strong Earnings; CarMax and Others on Deck
items.summary: Jabil ($JBL) exceeded earnings expectations with adjusted EPS up 37% year-over-year. CarMax ($KMX) is projected to report earnings before Wednesday's opening bell, alongside Smith & Wesson Brands ($SWBI) and Safe Bulkers ($SB).

items.headline: May Retail Sales Show Solid Growth
items.summary: May retail sales rose 0.9% month-on-month, surpassing analyst expectations. Even excluding vehicles and gasoline, retail sales showed solid growth, indicating resilient consumer spending.
